coredata.dc:title: Titanium-boride eutectic materials: Phase equilibria and properties of alloys in the Ti-rich portion of the Ti–Sn–B system
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2005 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Bondar, A.A.
Velikanova, T.Ya.
Borysov, D.B.
Artyukh, L.V.
Bilous, O.O.
Burka, M.P.
Fomichov, O.S.
Tsyganenko, N.I.
Firstov, S.O.
coredata.dc:description: Phase equilibria in the Ti–TiB–Ti3Sn region of the Ti–Sn–B system were experimentally investigated in the melting (solidification) temperature range using metallography (including EPMA), XRD, DTA, and pyrometry. The phase diagram is presented as solidus and liquidus surfaces projections and a vertical section through the 7.5at.% B. The Sn alloying was found to have a profound hardening effect on the eutectic titanium-boride alloys.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Eutectic solidification
Differential thermal analysis (DTA)
Titanium alloy
Hardness
Boride reinforcement

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of phase transformation temperatures of the binary Ti–Sn alloys [4,6] and those of the metal matrix saturated with boron in the as-cast Ti–Sn–B alloys. 1, ternary alloys Ti–Sn–7.5B (at.%); 2, binary alloy Ti81.5Sn18.5.

The Ti–TiB–Ti3Sn phase diagram: (a) solidus projection, (b) melting diagram projection. 1, EPMA data for samples annealed at subsolidus temperatures; 2 and 3, composition of two- and three-phase alloys.

Micrographs of Ti–Sn–B as-cast alloys (SEM images in secondary electron): a, Ti89.9Sn2.6B7.5, two-phase (Ti,Sn)+TiB; b, Ti82.5Sn10B7.5, two-phase (Ti,Sn)+TiB; c, Ti79.5Sn13B7.5, three-phase (Ti,Sn)+TiB+Ti3Sn.
XRD patterns of the ternary three-phase (Ti,Sn)+TiB+Ti3Sn alloy Ti74Sn18.5B7.5, as-cast (a) and annealed at 1500°C for 4h (b), and binary two-phase (Ti,Sn)+Ti3Sn alloy Ti81.5Sn18.5 (c).
Lattice parameters of (αTi,Sn) phase vs. Sn content in alloys Ti–Sn–7.5at.% B. 1, as-cast; 2, annealed at 1500°C for 4h.

Differential heating curves of the comparative DTA experiments (crucibles of HfO2, heating rates of 40°C/min): (A) 1, the binary alloy Ti81.5Sn18.5 is in the place of test sample, and Ti79.5Sn13B7.5 is in the place of reference substance; 2, the alloys places are exchanged. The ternary alloy was primarily melted in the both cases. (B) 1, the two-phase (Ti,Sn)+TiB alloy Ti82.5Sn10B7.5 is in the place of test sample, and the three-phase (Ti,Sn)+TiB+Ti3Sn alloy Ti79.5Sn13B7.5 is in the place of reference substance; 2, the alloys places are exchanged. The two-phase (Ti,Sn)+TiB alloy Ti82.5Sn10B7.5 w as primarily melted in the both cases.

The vertical section of the phase diagram through the 7.5at.% B isoconcentrate. 1, thermal arrests on DTA heating curves; 2, melting points from Pirani & Alterthum technique measurements; 3 and 4, two- and three-phase alloys after the 1500°C annealing.

Temperature dependence of hardness for as-cast alloys Ti–Sn–7.5B (at.%), HV vs. T (a) and ln(HV) vs. −1/T (b). 1, Ti92.5B7.5; 2, Ti91.6Sn0.9B7.5; 3, Ti89.9Sn2.6B7.5; 4, Ti87.4Sn5.1B7.5; 5, Ti74Sn18.5B7.5.
Hardness vs. boron additions for as-cast alloys Ti–Sn–7.5B (at.%) at selected temperatures. 1, room temperature; 2, 300°C; 3, 500°C; 4, 700°C.
Crystal structure data [4] and lattice parameters from the current work for the relevant Ti–Sn–B phases

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Averaged for the three-phase alloys (Ti,Sn)+Ti3Sn+TiB, Ti79.5Sn13B7.5 and Ti74Sn18.5B7.5.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structure and phase constituents of the as-cast and annealed Ti–Sn–B alloys

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: β is the β titanium phase transformed to α.

article.ce:title: Titanium-boride eutectic materials: Phase equilibria and properties of alloys in the Ti-rich portion of the Ti–Sn–B system

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Frantsevich Institute for Problems of Materials Science, Kyiv 03680, Ukraine

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Phase equilibria in the Ti–TiB–Ti3Sn region of the Ti–Sn–B system were experimentally investigated in the melting (solidification) temperature range using metallography (including EPMA), XRD, DTA, and pyrometry. The phase diagram is presented as solidus and liquidus surfaces projections and a vertical section through the 7.5at.% B. The Sn alloying was found to have a profound hardening effect on the eutectic titanium-boride alloys.

ce:section.ce:para: Tin is a conventional alloying addition for titanium alloys. The interest in titanium-boride materials appeared in the last decade necessitates information gaining on the influence of Sn alloying on the constitution and properties of titanium-boride eutectic alloys as in situ composites. The effect of tin additives on the mechanical properties of pure titanium was studied in [1–3] and the significant increase in strength was found to occur at alloying with 3–6at.% Sn at room temperature (RT) as well as at 400°C. The objectives of the current work are to study phase equilibria within the Ti–TiB–Ti3Sn region in the melting (solidification) range and, in parallel, to estimate the Sn effect on Vickers hardness from RT to 800°C for titanium-boride eutectic alloys.

The starting materials used in this investigation were iodide titanium (99.9wt.% purity), tin (99.9995wt.%), and boron (initial powder boron contained 5.27wt.% O, 0.55wt.% H, less than 0.005wt.% N, about 10−1 wt.% Cu, less than 10−2 wt.% Fe, and less than 10−2 wt.% Si). In order to escape inserting volatile contaminations into alloys, the initial boron powder was previously melted alone in an arc furnace and then crushed to grits. Boron and tin were inserted as components of master alloys Ti75B25 and Ti75Sn25, which were first melted from the starting materials under the same conditions as later used for preparing alloys. Chemical analyses of Ti75B25 and Ti75Sn25 showed the oxygen contamination to be 0.11 and 0.12wt.% (the technique used reducing extraction in a Ni bath followed by chromatographic identification), respectively, and Ti75B25 to contain 10−2 wt.% Si, 10−2 wt.% Fe, 10−3 wt.% Al, and 10−3 wt.% Mn (from atomic absorption spectroscopy with electrospark activation). So, comparing with the initial boron, the level of oxygen and other contamination was sufficiently low in Ti75B25 and in the ternary alloys (e.g. 0.04wt.% O was contained in the alloy Ti89.9Sn2.6B7.5).

A wet chemical analysis showed the Ti75B25 master alloy to contain 26at.% B, which corresponds to the intended composition within the accuracy of boron estimation (5% of its content). Since the mass losses of the Ti75Sn25 master alloy on melting were small, 45mg on 15g buttons on average (0.3wt.%), its composition was accepted as nominal.

The alloys under study were melted in the laboratory arc furnace with a nonconsumable tungsten electrode on a water-cooled copper hearth under an argon atmosphere (pressure ∼80kPa) that had previously been gettered by melting titanium for 5min. Charges of 15g (weighing errors were within 1mg) from iodide titanium (cuts) and the master alloys (grits) were fused with a weak arc and then melted six times with flipping between meltings. Cooling rates were estimated to be about 100°C/s. The total weight losses of the ingots on melting were always less than 0.3wt.%, hence, nominal compositions were adopted.

The as-cast and annealed samples were studied by means of metallography and XRD, mainly with a DRON-3 diffractometer on sections (excepting brittle samples, Ti75Sn25 and alloys at 25at.% B, which were taken from powders). The temperatures of the α→β transformations and the melting points were determined by DTA (crucibles from Al2O3 and HfO2, heating/cooling rate of about 40 or 50°C/min) on heating curves, owing to the fact that some melt/crucible reaction inevitably occurs. The pyrometric measurements, after Pirani & Althertum, were used to determine the temperature of the solidus (incipient melting). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and electron microprobe analyses (EPMA) were performed on a JEOL Superprobe 733 instrument supplemented with WLS equipment. The Ti75Sn25 binary master alloy was used as an EPMA standard. Measurement corrections were made with a standard ZAF procedure. Three to five individual analyses were carried out for each phase in a sample. Since errors in determining B contents considerably exceed the errors for the metals, only the contents of metals (their errors came within ∼1at.%) were measured and used for phase diagram construction. More experimental details on the DTA and pyrometry used are available elsewhere [7].

Vickers microhardness was measured after a 0.49N loading at RT and Vickers hardness HV was after a 9.8N loading in vacuum at temperatures from RT to 800°C. The hot Vickers hardness was used as express-estimation of alloy strength in the wide temperature range, taking into account the fact that Velikanova et al. [8] had shown Hill's ratio [9] between hardness and strength HV=3σ 0.2 to hold true for the present class of alloys.

Metallographic examination of as-cast alloys showed that the characteristic structure of the binary (Ti)+TiB eutectic is conserved in the whole two-phase region (Ti,Sn)+TiB of the ternary system (Fig. 3 a and b), which reaches up to 10at.% Sn at 7.5at.% B. The micrographs reveal the needle shaped TiB whiskers randomly distributed in the (Ti,Sn) matrix, which is a hereditary βTi-based phase transformed into the αTi-based one. In the three-phase alloys (Ti,Sn)+Ti3Sn+TiB (Fig. 3c) the TiB boride grows in the same morphology to form as a primary and/or eutectic phase during solidification.

Another feature of the as-cast alloys in the Ti–TiB–Ti3Sn region is the absence of essential liquation. Therefore, the alloys reached equilibrium state during the used annealings and some substantial evidences can prove this conclusion, such as coarsening of the (Ti,Sn)+TiB eutectic structure and approximately identical peaks in XRD patterns of as-cast and annealed alloys (Fig. 4 ). In contrast to this, the alloys Ti61.5Sn13.5B25 and Ti70.2Sn22.3B7.5, which are out of the Ti–TiB–Ti3Sn area mentioned, contained four phases, Ti3Sn, TiB, Ti3B4, and TiB2, in both as-cast state and after annealing at 1500°C for 4h. The 8h annealing at 1500°C leaded to the three-phase state Ti3Sn+TiB+Ti3B4 of the alloy Ti61.5Sn13.5B25, while in the latter Ti70.2Sn22.3B7.5 all the four phases remained.

The phase based on the bcc βTi was never observed in the X-ray patterns obtained, only the hcp (αTi,Sn), Ti3Sn and titanium borides were identified (Fig. 4). As seen in Fig. 5 , within the two-phase field (αTi,Sn)+TiB, the (αTi,Sn) lattice parameters increase with increasing Sn content in the alloys Ti–Sn–7.5at.% B to ∼10at.% (i.e. to ∼12at.% Sn in the (Ti,Sn) matrix). This composition practically coincides with the solubility limit of Sn in the binary hcp (αTi,Sn) phase (Fig. 1). According to the EPMA data, the titanium matrix in the three-phase (αTi,Sn)+TiB+Ti3Sn alloys annealed at 1500°C contains ∼13.5at.% Sn (assuming ∼14at.% Sn at the solidus temperature 1575°C). The Sn contents obtained are in reasonable accordance with the binary phase diagram [4] (the maximal Sn solubility in (βTi,Sn) being 17.5at.% Sn). The EPMA data show low Sn solubility in the TiB boride, not exceeding 0.1at.%. The Ti3Sn lattice parameters of the binary (Ti,Sn)+Ti3Sn and three-phase (Ti,Sn)+TiB+Ti3Sn ternary alloys are the same within the estimation errors (Table 1), which leads to the conclusion on the negligible boron solubility in this phase.

Based on the obtained data, the phase diagram in the Ti–TiB–Ti3Sn region was constructed for the melting temperature range as solidus and liquidus surfaces projections (Fig. 2). As seen the Sn alloying increases the melting points of the two-phase eutectic (βTi,Sn)+TiB alloys. Since the temperatures of the binary invariant three-phase equilibrium le ⇔(βTi,Sn)+Ti3Sn and invariant four-phase equilibrium of liquid, (βTi,Sn), TiB, and Ti3Sn are rather close, only comparative DTA experiments could give data for reaching a conclusion on the character of the four-phase equilibrium. The melting point of the binary two-phase (Ti,Sn)+Ti3Sn alloy Ti81.5Sn18.5 is obviously higher than that of the ternary three-phase (Ti,Sn)+TiB+Ti3Sn alloy Ti79.5Sn13B7.5 (Fig. 6 A). The latter melts after melting of the two-phase (Ti,Sn)+TiB alloy Ti82.5Sn10B7.5 (Fig. 6B). This was the main reason why the invariant four-phase equilibrium at 1575°C was assigned to the reaction LU3 +Ti3Sn⇔(βTi,Sn)+TiB. The liquidus curve of the monovariant eutectic reaction L⇔(βTi,Sn)+TiB goes down from the U3 point to the point e2 (le ⇔(βTi)+TiB) in the binary Ti–B system (Fig. 2b) without any extremum in the melting trough. At the same time the α→β transformation temperatures were insufficiently affected by boron alloying, as clearly seen in Fig. 1 where the transformation temperatures of the metal matrix (Ti,Sn) saturated with boron are compared with the binary Ti–Sn phase diagram from [4]. So, in the vertical section through the 7.5at.% B isoconcentrate (Fig. 7 ) the phase equilibria involving (βTi,Sn) and (αTi,Sn) were assumed to be similar to the binary system.

Fig. 8 presents the hot Vickers hardness HV of the as-cast eutectic alloys (Ti,Sn)+TiB (curves 1–4). As seen in Figs. 8 and 9 , it gradually increases in the whole temperature interval under investigation with increasing Sn content. It should be mainly attributed to hardening of the metal matrix on Sn alloying since the microstructure of the ternary two-phase alloys (Ti,Sn)+TiB is essentially the same as for the binary system Ti–B and the TiB boride does practically not solve any Sn. This conclusion was also confirmed by microhardness measurements. For both the metal matrix (βTi,Sn), transformed into (αTi,Sn), and the eutectic (βTi,Sn)+TiB, transformed into (αTi,Sn)+TiB, the microhardness increases with increasing Sn content, from 1.8 and 2.3GPa to 3.2 and 4.1GPa at 10at.% Sn, respectively. In the alloy Ti94Sn18.5B7.5 the appearance of additional reinforcing phase Ti3Sn, together with TiB, results in a substantial increase in hardness (Fig. 8, curve 5).

In order to determine more precisely the influence of the alloying additions on the temperature of incipient sharp softening, the curves HV versus temperature (T) were replotted in the form lg(HV) versus −1/T (Fig. 8b). As it is seen, there are three linear ranges that correspond to the three apparent activation energies. The physical nature of each region can be interpreted in terms of the considerations presented in Ref. [10]. The activation energy of flow during Vickers pyramid submerging in a sample was found to be 6–7kJ/mole for the low temperature interval, from RT to 250–450°C, where the overcoming of long-range elastic fields of dislocations controls the material flow. At intermediate temperatures, ∼300 to 500–700°C the energy is about 20–40kJ/mole, corresponding to interaction of dislocations with interstitial atoms. At higher temperatures the activation energy is ∼120kJ/mole (up to ∼160kJ/mole for alloys rich in Sn), which coincides with the data on the interface diffusion for αTi [11,12]. Of the two breakings, the second one is of special interest as an incipient temperature of sharp softening (so-called “strength break-down” temperature). As seen in Fig. 8b, the “strength break-down” temperature is markedly affected by Sn alloying. It increases from 500 to 660°C with increasing Sn content in the metal matrix up to 6at.% and even up to 700°C for the alloy with the matrix saturated with Sn.

Based on the results of this investigation, the following are the key observations:
(i)
The two-phase (Ti,Sn)+TiB field, being of interest for the development of titanium-boride composites, is restricted to the tie-line (Ti∼86Sn∼14)-TiB at the solidus temperature 1575°C.
(ii)
Tin can be considered as a desirable alloying addition for the titanium-boride eutectic alloys as it is known to be useful for conventional titanium materials, raising high-temperature strength properties and increasing the sharp softening temperature.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Air Force Office of Scientific Research (USA) under the STCU Project P-060. The authors thank Dr. D. Miracle (Air Force Lab.) for the initiation of this research and helpful discussions of the results. Thanks to Prof. J. Perepezko (University of Wisconsin, Madison) for useful remarks. Participation of Dr. P. Martsenyuk and Mr. A. Samelyuk (Frantsevich Institute for Problems of Materials Science) in experimental works is appreciated.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Phase Diagrams of Binary Titanium Alloys
Phase Diagrams of Binary Titanium Alloys
Metallic Materials with High Structural Efficiency
